name: Orang Bunian
description: The Orang Bunian, meaning 'Hidden People' or 'Elven People', are supernatural beings in Malay, Indonesian, and Bornean folklore. They are believed to inhabit invisible realms parallel to our own, often residing in dense forests, ancient trees, or abandoned houses. Orang Bunian are generally described as beautiful and fair-skinned, with a refined and elegant demeanor. They possess advanced knowledge of herbal medicine, magic, and craftsmanship, and are said to be skilled artisans, particularly in creating exquisite jewelry and textiles. While often benevolent and helpful, offering assistance to those lost in the forest or rewarding kindness, they can also be mischievous or even dangerous if offended. Stories abound of humans being lured into their world, sometimes returning years later with no memory of their time, or marrying Orang Bunian and becoming trapped in their realm. The Orang Bunian serve as a reminder of the delicate balance between the human world and the unseen forces of nature, urging respect for the environment and adherence to traditional customs. Their existence highlights the rich animistic beliefs woven into the cultural fabric of Southeast Asia.
subtitle: Hidden People of the Forest
appearance: Orang Bunian are typically described as beautiful and fair-skinned, often resembling humans but with more refined features and an ethereal glow. Their hair is often long and flowing, and they are said to wear elegant clothing made of natural materials. Some accounts describe them as being slightly shorter than average humans. They are often associated with the colors gold and white, symbolizing their purity and connection to the spirit world.
habitat: Orang Bunian are said to inhabit dense forests, ancient trees, abandoned houses, caves, and hidden realms parallel to the human world. They are often found in areas considered sacred or untouched by human development. Specific locations mentioned in folklore include Mount Ledang and Taman Negara in Malaysia, and various forests throughout Indonesia and Borneo.
behavior: Orang Bunian can be both benevolent and mischievous. They are known to help those who are lost or in need, often guiding them back to safety or providing them with food and shelter. They appreciate kindness and respect for nature, and may reward those who exhibit these qualities. However, they are easily offended by disrespect, greed, or arrogance, and may punish those who cross them with illness, misfortune, or even by luring them into their world. They are also known for their love of music and dance, and are said to hold elaborate feasts and celebrations in their hidden realms.

keyMyths.title: The Lost Child
keyMyths.description: A child wanders into the forest and is lost. An Orang Bunian guides the child back home, but the child returns with strange gifts and stories of a hidden world.

keyMyths.title: The Enchanted Marriage
keyMyths.description: A human falls in love with an Orang Bunian and marries them, entering their world. However, they eventually long for their old life and must find a way to return, often facing difficult choices.

sources.author: Sir Richard Winstedt
sources.description: A classic anthropological study of Malay beliefs and practices, including the Orang Bunian.

sources.author: Rosemary Ellen Guiley
sources.work: The Encyclopedia of Demons and Demonology
sources.description: Provides a brief overview of the Orang Bunian in the context of Southeast Asian folklore.
